chapters.title: Even on dark days, we have the power to see the light
chapters.text:
Politics in the USA has always been divisive. There will always be significant differences of opinion between parties, and it’s impossible to please everyone with decisions made. Yet, we are living through a period of political change that seems dark and confusing to most.

You could argue that life is going backward rather than forward. From rolling back women’s rights to cracking down hard on immigration, the state of the economy isn’t the only thing on the minds of American citizens.

Jon Meacham has studied US history for years and has noticed many patterns that seem to repeat themselves time and time again. While the current situation may seem dark to many, the positive thing is that there have been dark times in the past, and they’ve always been conquered. Rather than allowing hate to divide and negativity to rule, Meacham suggests we look for lessons and make changes, focusing on hope and positivity instead.

The four years that Donald Trump sat in office have created a situation like no other. Those who previously felt alienated and pushed to one side experienced a fresh wave of energy and mobilized in large numbers. Meacham points out that even David Duke of the KKK said he would vote for Trump. You know the situation isn’t so good when you hear things like that.

Fear and hate have been prevalent in US history before. Still, rather than committing it to the history books and allowing it to remain there, it’s a far better idea to use these situations as yardsticks to avoid. Such periods may be turbulent, but they can be survived.

One positive is that the population will always speak up for what is right. People will keep fighting for better days ahead. We need to join the fight and allow our voices to be heard.

It’s easy to assume that the best days are always behind us, but the future can be brighter if we learn the lessons of the past and keep hoping for something more positive.

In this summary, you will learn about some notable events in US history and how they still affect modern-day America. Perhaps understanding history a little better will allow more eyes to open to potential mistakes in the here and now.

chapters.title: Change happens when people are brave enough to speak up
chapters.text:
Whenever a significant change happens, it’s rarely down to those in power. The background often stems from people mobilizing and making noise.

Having the bravery to stand up for what you believe in isn’t easy. You will never find that 100% of people will agree with you, and you may open yourself up to major disagreements with those around you, but it’s always better than staying quiet and thinking, ‘what if.’

In the history of America, we have seen time and time again how change comes when people rise up and speak out. The women’s suffrage movement is one such example. It may have taken many years to be heard and taken seriously, but the change was made with a commitment to the cause and perseverance. Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Lucretia Mott, and Susan B. Anthony were the three founders of the movement who refused to be silenced. Had they not been brave and pushed through boundaries, perhaps women would still be denied the right to vote even today.

Sojourner Truth was an activist who refused to be silenced even though speaking out was extremely dangerous for her. Having been born into slavery, she escaped and became an inspiration for countless others. By using her voice and refusing to bow to extreme pressure, she pushed for not only the rights of women but the abolishment of slavery.

Citizens rising up and refusing to be silenced is critical, but for change to be made official, presidents need to take concrete action.

Abraham Lincoln signed the order to abolish slavery, and Woodrow Wilson supported women’s voting rights. Yet, without that push from the people, it’s likely that change would have been delayed or perhaps never made at all.

There is a lesson to be learned here. While making your voice heard may seem complicated and sometimes scary, it’s necessary. The road to reform is long, and there are bumps along the way. But, by staying silent, you’re complicit, which means you do not have the right to complain either.

Does reform mean the problem is solved? No. Even though Abraham Lincoln signed the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863 that abolished slavery, racism in modern-day America is still rife. There is still more work to be done.

chapters.title: Immigration has long been a subject of division in the US
chapters.text:
Many countries are grappling with rising immigration levels, yet America has always been a country of immigrants. This hasn’t occurred recently; it’s been the case since the US was founded.

Regardless of your opinion on immigration, we can quickly see how this issue has caused ripples throughout history.

Theodore Roosevelt made the first steps toward limiting immigration to the US. However, his stance on the subject and his actions often clashed.

Roosevelt was initially asked about a play he went to see called The Melting Pot. The play was about a Russian/Jewish immigrant family, and Roosevelt stated that he felt outraged that a man would be judged based on his place of birth or religion. However, despite those public words, his actions clashed. He felt that too many people were traveling to the US and that action needed to be taken. Stances such as this often fuel hatred amongst those who disagree with immigration laws, pushing a view that can cause violence and discord.

During Donald Trump’s tenure, we saw a rise in white supremacy, fuelled by inflammatory rhetoric. Yet again, we haven’t learned a key lesson.

Roosevelt’s words showed that he didn’t have the correct grasp on immigration. He assumed that rich, highly skilled individuals would travel to the US, adding value to the workforce. However, we know that is often not the case when individuals need to flee their country of birth and seek a better life elsewhere.

The Immigration Act of 1907 was passed on February 20 and aimed to restrict the number of immigrants entering the country. It focused on reducing the number of Japanese immigrants, along with those with a disability, illness, or women who were traveling for purposes claimed to be ‘immoral.’

Americanism is a question of spirit, conviction, and purpose, not of creed or birthplace. ~ Theodore Roosevelt

However, Roosevelt wanted anyone who wished to enter the country to give their all to the country and flag. It was about allegiance to America and caused upset amongst those who still retained parts of their own cultures from overseas.

Did you know? In 2021, 45.4 million immigrants (those born outside of the US) lived in America.

chapters.title: The women’s suffrage movement succeeded at a time when speech was restricted
chapters.text:
The women’s suffrage movement is a monumental piece of progress in US history. However, it occurred at a time when freedom of speech was restricted and speaking out was considered extremely risky.

During the height of the women’s rights movement, Woodrow Wilson was President. Throughout the year, he hadn’t seemed to be much of a supporter of the movement, yet the ferocity of feeling seen in rallies and protests up and down the country told him that he couldn’t wait to act for a second longer.

However, on September 30, 1918, Woodrow Wilson walked into Capitol Hill and delivered a speech to give his support to the women’s rights movements and backed an amendment to the Constitution. The Great War was raging worldwide, and Wilson stated that throughout such troubled times, the world looked toward America as a beaming light of hope. Such upsetting times required inclusion and equality, not division and hate.

Recognizing women’s rights was a long-overdue decision, yet we still have a long way to go regarding equality, even today.

On the same day, there was a massive demonstration outside Capitol Hill. Men shouted insults at those participating in the rally, yet there was joy when the amendment was passed on August 18, 1920. It took decades of noise and persistence, but as we’ve mentioned before, rising up peacefully and firmly has resulted in change, time and time again.

This was all the more monumental because freedom of speech, one of the cornerstones of the American Constitution, had been restricted due to national security reasons. When America entered the Great War, a bill was passed to make it illegal to speak or write negatively about the US and its war efforts.

Eugene V. Debs, the leader of the Socialist Party, was jailed for ten years due to an anti-war speech, suggesting that America’s involvement in the war was simply a ruse to increase imperialism.

The lesson here? Speaking out is risky, but it can bring about a welcome change.

chapters.title: Overcoming racism in America requires decisive action
chapters.text:
Many years after the abolishment of slavery, the US still has a significant problem with racism. It's very easy to look back through history and see how views have been shaped by events and passed down through generations. However, it's equally important to note that everyone is responsible for their opinions and how they treat others.

Every president has grappled with racism issues, and while progress has been made, more is needed. Racism is an issue that requires a significant overhaul of mindset and culture.

Harry Truman started to take steps toward equality and fairness by issuing executive orders on civil rights. Despite his actions, Truman himself had made racial remarks while being a descendant of slave owners. However, upon seeing the country’s state and events at the time, he decided that it was his duty as president to make changes.

My forebears were Confederates … But my very stomach turned over when I learned that Negro soldiers, just back from overseas, were being dumped out of Army trucks in Mississippi and being beaten. ~ Harry Truman

Truman made several statements about equal rights for everyone on voting, housing, healthcare, and employment. Many citizens living in the country’s south opposed his views and made their voices heard. However, Truman reacted with fury.

Despite his previous views, he made it clear that the past could not be allowed to continue. Upon being confronted by a group of angry citizens, he quoted a section from the Constitution that spoke about equal rights. He stated that the bill doesn’t only apply to white people but to every single person in the land.

Today, we see an America that is much improved yet nowhere near the level it should be. We still see attacks on immigrants and racially motivated violence. Trying to defeat racism is a struggle that lives on, and Jon Meacham points out that we cannot ignore the fact that history has shaped the country as it is today.

chapters.title: Past mistakes should not continue to drag the country down
chapters.text:
Meacham talks about critical moments in America’s history, yet he makes it very clear that in order to move forward and create new and positive change, we must look for lessons and take action.

Far too often, we look at past Presidents and place them on a pedestal, but Presidents are human beings too; they’re just like all of us. They make mistakes, they have good ideas, and they take action that’s questionable sometimes and fantastic at others.

It’s essential to hold those in power accountable and not idolize them simply because of their position. Nobody is perfect, and we shouldn’t look at the history of America and hold it in high esteem either. There have been significant movements toward positive change, but big mistakes have also occurred. The question is, what can we learn from, and what should we see as an example of good?

It’s essential to look at the action we’re taking today and ask what those yet to come would think of it. What will the history books say about the current period we’re living in?

During a eulogy to victims of a white supremacist attack in Charleston, South Carolina, President Obama said, “For too long, we’ve been blind to the way past injustices continue to shape the present.” It’s important to be firm and ask searching questions to make positive change.

Three other important strategies include:

Avoiding tribal behavior. During turbulent times, many people become motivated by the other person’s view, which they assume is extremism. As a result, groups form an almost ‘us and them’ mentality.

Respect all views. Facts are facts, but you can’t ignore people’s opinions either. To live harmoniously, we need to look around us and avoid tunnel vision, i.e., seeing only our side of the argument.

Understand that those in power are human. Yes, Presidents and politicians are in a position of power and need to do their best, but know that they’re human and can make mistakes.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The history books will always document the significant points of days gone by, but living through those days cannot be written accurately. Can you imagine living when women had almost zero rights? Can you imagine having to sit at the back of the bus and giving up your seat to a white person?

America is by no means perfect, but there has been progress. In terms of women’s rights and racism, there is more work to be done. Presidents don’t always get it right, but the power is really in the people’s hands. Standing up, shouting about what is essential, and making your voice heard, are all things which push issues to the front of the agenda and force action to be made.

History continues to shape modern-day America. You can see it in every major issue that still exists in society today. According to Jon Meacham, the Trump era stoked many divisions we’ve seen occur before, but time will tell whether new administrations can move forward to brighter days.

The plus point is that all the information is there for us to learn from. We can look at past mistakes and ensure that the same thing doesn’t happen again. We can work carefully to navigate similar problems and seek to discover different ways to keep making progress. The problematic issue is that past Presidents haven’t done that. Instead, they’ve allowed the same problems to crop up and cause discord.

Something has to change.

Try this

• What do you feel passionate about? Can you learn more and perhaps join groups to lobby the Government and make changes?
• Take the time to learn about the critical points in American history and understand how these events may have shaped your viewpoints.
• Are you voting for the right person? Before the next election, do your due diligence and inform yourself on key issues. Then, you can make a decision that aligns with your values.

Read it till the end?
